papers.title: Real-Time AI Service Economy: A Framework for Agentic Computing Across the Continuum
papers.innovation: Proposes a comprehensive framework for orchestrating real-time AI services and autonomous agents across the device-edge-cloud continuum, tackling critical challenges like latency, resource competition, and policy/governance constraints in distributed environments.
papers.benchmarks: Addresses system-level performance (latency, throughput), resource utilization, and policy adherence for real-time, distributed agentic workloads, implicitly suggesting new metrics for resilience and scalability in production AI systems.
papers.use_case: Designing, implementing, and managing robust, scalable, and governed real-time AI service architectures and autonomous agent deployments in complex, distributed computing environments (e.g., IoT, edge computing).

papers.title: The World Won't Stay Still: Programmable Evolution for Agent Benchmarks
papers.innovation: Introduces 'programmable evolution' for agent benchmarks, addressing the critical limitation of static environments in current LLM agent evaluations. This enables testing agent robustness and adaptability to dynamically changing real-world conditions.
papers.benchmarks: Directly focuses on the creation of new, dynamic benchmarks that evolve over time (e.g., changing schemas, toolsets, user requests), thereby allowing for more realistic and stringent evaluation of LLM agent robustness and generalization capabilities.
papers.use_case: Developing and evaluating more robust, adaptable, and reliable LLM-powered agents for real-world deployment, and constructing next-generation agent evaluation platforms that simulate dynamic environments.

papers.title: Agentic LLM Planning via Step-Wise PDDL Simulation: An Empirical Characterisation
papers.innovation: Provides an empirical characterization of Large Language Models (LLMs) as viable planners for autonomous systems, leveraging step-wise PDDL (Planning Domain Definition Language) simulation to rigorously assess their task planning capabilities.
papers.benchmarks: Utilizes standard PDDL planning problems as a formal benchmark to evaluate LLMs' ability to generate correct, complete, and efficient action sequences for complex tasks, comparing their performance against classical symbolic methods.
papers.use_case: Integrating LLMs into autonomous robotic systems, complex software agents, or other applications requiring formal task planning; understanding and improving LLM capabilities for logical sequencing and problem-solving in goal-oriented tasks.
